title: Explore: Official GESP scope
description: Compare GESP level 5 syllabus with the user's DP example and identify level boundaries.
findings: Official C++/Python level 5 covers elementary number theory, high precision, linked lists, binary search/answer, recursion, divide and conquer, greedy, and complexity. Simple DP is level 6; complex DP is level 7.
recommendations: Treat level+algorithm queries as syllabus-aware, with out-of-level warnings.

title: Explore: Problem source acquisition
description: Identify viable sources for GESP problems, metadata, and topic evidence.
findings: Primary source should be the official GESP site: syllabus PDF, true-question index, and per-exam PDFs. Secondary sources can map OJ IDs and community tags, but must be marked as non-official.
recommendations: Build a source registry with license, robots, rate limit, parser type, and attribution rules.

title: Explore: Product concept
description: Analyze product surface for GESP problem-type classification and learning navigation.
findings: The core product is a classified knowledge graph: level -> algorithm domain -> problem type -> problem -> knowledge point -> source evidence.
recommendations: Ship classified catalog pages, source alignment, and admin validation before personalization.

title: Score: Feasibility
description: Score feasibility based on data availability, parsing difficulty, and product scope.
findings: Feasible if the MVP starts with official PDF ingestion and semi-automatic tagging. Full crawler coverage and auto-tag accuracy require iterative validation.
recommendations: Start with C++ level 5 and official PDFs; add Python and community mirrors later.

title: Score: Impact
description: Score user and business value for parents, students, and teachers.
findings: High value because it turns scattered PDFs/OJ/problem solutions into a classified exam map and exposes syllabus mismatch, frequency, and practice routes.
recommendations: Position as exam-prep intelligence rather than just a problem list.

title: Score: Risk
description: Score risks from copyright, source drift, classifier errors, and syllabus mismatch.
findings: Main risks are republishing copyrighted content, ambiguous community tags, PDF extraction noise, and official syllabus vs real-question boundary cases.
recommendations: Store metadata and excerpts by default; link to sources; keep human review and evidence-level confidence.

title: Score: Complexity
findings: This is a multi-subsystem project: source registry, crawler/parser, problem alignment, classifier, catalog UI, and admin review.
recommendations: Classify as large scope and split into roadmap milestones.

title: Score: Alignment
description: Score alignment with the stated goal.
findings: Strong alignment: the product answers 'for each GESP level and algorithm domain, which problem types, problems, and knowledge points are covered'.
recommendations: Keep the first UX centered on classified catalog pages: level, algorithm domain, problem type, problem detail, and source alignment.

title: Score: Maintainability
findings: Maintainability depends on provenance-first data modeling and separating official facts from inferred/community labels.
recommendations: Use versioned source documents, tag evidence records, and reviewer overrides.

title: Decision Synthesis
description: Compile conclusions, locked/free/deferred decisions, scope verdict, and next-step routing.
findings: Go. Scope verdict is large because it spans source registry, crawler, parser, canonical problem alignment, classifier, classified catalog UI, and review workflow. Next step should be roadmap or blueprint.
